screen.name: screen1
screen.instruct:
As a standard, we’ll use codeine-d3, which is a modified form of the opiate codeine. If this standard shows up later in our graphs, we’ll know that the test worked.
Use the pipette to add a specific amount of standard to each vial.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control
Codeine-d3

screen.name: screen2
screen.instruct:
As a positive control, we’ll use a mixture of opiates in known concentrations.
Use the pipette to add a specific amount of the opiate mix to the vial labeled “positive control”.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control
Opiate mix

screen.name: screen3
screen.instruct: Good. Now you can add a specific amount of blood to the vial labeled “blood”.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control

screen.name: screen4
screen.instruct: Lastly, you need to fill up all three vials with a specific amount of diluting solution.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control
Diluting solution

screen.name: screen5
screen.instruct: Place all three vials in the gas chromatograph.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control

screen.name: screen6
screen.instruct: Let me explain the process while we’re waiting for the results.

screen.name: screen7
screen.instruct:
First, the chemicals in the sample are separated based on their properties.
This part is the same as the regular gas chromatography you already know from analyzing the flooring samples.

screen.name: screen8
screen.instruct:
The gas chromatograph is connected to a mass spectrometer, which will help us identify the unknown chemical.
The first step is that each chemical in the sample gets a positive charge in the ion source and breaks into fragments.
screen.caption: ION SOURCE

screen.name: screen9
screen.instruct: The analyzer sorts the fragments of each chemical based on their properties.
screen.caption: ANALYZER

screen.name: screen10
screen.instruct: Finally, the detector adds up the number of fragments of each type.
screen.caption: DETECTOR

screen.name: screen11
screen.instruct: The results are ready. Click on the monitor to analyze the data.

screen.name: screen12
screen.instruct:
First, we’ll look at the gas chromatogram, which shows the chemicals as they came out of the gas chromatograph column.
Looks like we have two different chemicals in our sample. Click on the first one to obtain the mass spectrum.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram blood sample
time (minutes)

screen.name: screen13
screen.instruct:
Graphs like this, called mass spectra, are generated for each chemical found in the sample.
Click on the terms "Mass spectrum" and "Peaks" to learn more about the graph.
screen.caption: Mass spectrum
of detected chemical #1
Peaks
Rel. Abundance
m/z
screen.spectrumPopup: The graph of a specific chemical created by the mass spectrometer.
screen.peaksPopup: Each fragment of a chemical appears as a separate peak. Each chemical has its own specific patterns of peaks.

screen.name: screen14
screen.instruct: It’s time to identify the first chemical. We’ll use our positive control to identify the chemicals in the blood.
screen.caption: Mass spectrum of detected chemical #1
Rel. Abundance
m/z

screen.name: screen15
screen.instruct:
Click the green arrows to tab through the positive control.
When you find a graph that matches our first mass spectrum, drag and drop the graph from the database onto our graph.
screen.caption: Mass spectrum of detected chemical #1
Rel. Abundance
m/z
POSITIVE CONTROL

screen.name: screen16
screen.instruct:
Now let’s identify the second chemical you found in the blood sample.
Click on the peak of the second chemical in the gas chromatogram to obtain the mass spectrum.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram blood sample
time (minutes)

screen.name: screen17
screen.instruct:
Click the green arrows to tab through the positive control.
When you find a graph that matches our second mass spectrum, drag and drop the graph from the database onto our graph.
screen.caption: Mass spectrum of detected chemical #2
Rel. Abundance
m/z
POSITIVE CONTROL
